text: The surgeon injects vasopressin, diluted with saline, into the posterior wall of the uterus where a nine-centimeter myoma is located, including part of the cervix.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to cut the serous layer of the uterus, which appears pale due to transient ischemia from vasopressin injection. A longitudinal incision is made and progressively enlarged to facilitate the enucleation of the myoma.

The surgeon uses a myoma screw from the right hand to facilitate the enucleation of the myoma, while holding a traumatic grasper and suction irrigation in the left hand. The myoma screw is preferred over scissors or Maryland due to the risk of jaw breakage. The instruments help achieve a good plane for easier removal of the myoma.

The surgeon uses suction irrigation to slowly separate the myoma from surrounding tissue. Dense attachments near the base are visible, and energy sources are employed to aid in the separation of feeding vessels.

The surgeon completes the separation of the last attachment of the myoma. Suturing and knotting are performed using a 40 mm needle to take deep bites through the muscle, closing the incision with either Mishra's knot or a square knot.

The surgeon ties multiple extracorporeal knots using a needle holder to provide tension and control bleeding. A square knot is used to secure the sutures, with deep bites taken to ensure closure of the muscle layer. The procedure concludes with a tubectomy, and additional square knots are tied to complete the suturing process.
The surgeon ties the fifth extracorporeal knot to secure the repair of the intramural myoma, which may require multiple layers of suturing. The myoma is then completely removed using morcellation.
